title: Numbers & Time
shortSummary: Count, tell the time, and talk about days in Hindi, essential for shopping, travel, and plans.
culturalNote: Hindi numbers are written with the Devanagari digits (० १ २ ३ ...) as well as the familiar 0-9, and you will see both on signs and price tags. India counts large amounts using lakh (लाख, one hundred thousand) and crore (करोड़, ten million) rather than millions, so 'do lakh' means two hundred thousand. Telling the time uses baje (बजे, o'clock), and the 24-hour clock is common in railway timetables, while everyday speech adds subah (morning), dopahar (afternoon), shaam (evening), and raat (night). India runs on a single time zone, IST, written as UTC+5:30, which is why the offset has the unusual half hour. Festivals such as Diwali and Holi follow the lunar Hindu calendar, so their dates shift each year against the Gregorian calendar.

grammar.explanation: A number sits before the noun, and many Hindi nouns do not add a separate plural ending the way English does. Do kitaab can mean two books; the number itself carries the plural sense, though the noun form may also shift in marked plurals.
grammar.commonMistake: Adding an English-style -s; Hindi changes the noun ending instead, e.g. kitaab to kitaaben.

grammar.explanation: To say the hour, put the number before baje (बजे, o'clock): teen baje (three o'clock). Saadhe (साढ़े) means 'half past', sava (सवा) means 'quarter past', and paune (पौने) means 'quarter to'.
grammar.commonMistake: Saying saadhe for one o'clock; 'half past one' is the irregular dedh baje.

dialogue.setup: A traveller asks a shopkeeper, Vinod, about the time and the price of fruit.

lines.speaker: Traveller
lines.native: धन्यवाद। ये आम कितने का है?
lines.transliteration: dhanyavaad. ye aam kitne ka hai?
lines.english: Thank you. How much is this mango?

lines.speaker: Vinod
lines.native: एक किलो साठ रुपये का है।
lines.transliteration: ek kilo saath rupaye ka hai.

lines.speaker: Vinod
lines.native: ठीक है, एक सौ बीस रुपये।
lines.transliteration: theek hai, ek sau bees rupaye.
lines.english: Okay, one hundred and twenty rupees.

quiz.question: How do you ask 'What time is it?' in Hindi?
quiz.explanation: Kitne means 'how many' and baje means 'o'clock'.

quiz.question: Which word means the number 'five'?
quiz.explanation: Paanch is five; teen is three, saat is seven, das is ten.

quiz.question: ये आम कितने _____ है? (How much is this mango?)
quiz.explanation: Ka is the postposition that links the price question to the masculine noun aam.

quiz.question: What does 'साढ़े चार बजे (saadhe chaar baje)' mean?
quiz.explanation: Saadhe means 'half past', so saadhe chaar is 4:30.

quiz.question: Arrange: बजे / हैं / तीन (It is three o'clock)
quiz.explanation: Number, then baje, then the verb hain at the end.
